narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 59.8% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction stemmed primarily from your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, since students who passed typically ranked around the 6th decile. Your LSAT score of 24 also contributed notably in the same direction, falling well below the average of about 37 for those who succeeded on the bar. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income bracket worked in your favor, as it pulled the prediction somewhat toward success compared to patterns seen in passers from varied income levels. Overall, these top three elements—your Year 3 decile most heavily, followed by your LSAT and then family income—shaped the model's cautious outlook more than other details like your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 or full-time study plans. While your undergraduate grades were slightly below the 3.24 average for bar passers, they weren't the main drivers here. Compared to typical admitted students, who often enter with stronger early rankings and higher LSATs aligning closer to 37, your profile signaled higher risk despite positives like being full-time, which is common among 93% of successful students. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background as an Asian female from a lower-middle income family—those details weren't factored into the prediction—but rather a data-driven assessment to help the school maintain strong bar passage rates. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your solid undergraduate performance and commitment to full-time study. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the 37 average, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or programs, and reapplying once you've addressed these key areas. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting these improvements, and we're here to support your next steps if you choose to strengthen your profile.
